bins.name: State-Federal Jurisdictional Balance
bins.desc: IECA urges collaboration between FERC and state regulators rather than jurisdictional conflict, but strongly opposes any mandatory federal process that overrides states where interconnection is already working. The filing sees joint cross-jurisdictional study as the solution in problem regions, with existing state processes preserved elsewhere.
quotes: Rather than spending valuable time fighting between the Commission and State regulators over jurisdictional boundaries, we urge all sides to work together to recognize and remedy those circumstances in which the process of interconnecting large loads to the interstate transmission system under State laws is taking an inordinate amount of time and seek ways to expedite the process for interconnecting large loads to the grid.

we also urge the Commission to recognize regional differences and not impose a new mandatory interconnection process on those regions experiencing rapid load growth from large loads, which are not experiencing interconnection challenges or delays.

We submit that a collaborative approach between States and the Commission can produce a process that can simultaneously study both a Data Center and a contractually-associated electric generation resource by joining both (i) the Federal approval of interconnection of the generation resource (through the Commission-regulated LGIP and LGIA process) and (ii) the State approval (through a State-regulated utility) of the interconnection of a new large load.

we suggest that any load interconnection program that the Commission may introduce should not be mandatory across the entirety of the U.S., i.e., a one-size-fits-all approach is not the best way forward.

bins.name: MW Threshold Too Low
bins.desc: IECA argues the proposed 20 MW threshold is too low: for speed-to-market, expedited review should apply to all loads regardless of size; for reliability purposes, the 20 MW floor is inconsequential and a 100 MW floor is more meaningful.
quotes: for purposes of expediting interconnection to the transmissions system (i.e., enhancing "speed to market"), we submit that expedited review and approval should be available to all loads (i.e., all Data Centers), not just large Data Centers in excess of any particular size.

Reliability and resource adequacy issues are affected much more by loads of 100 MW or larger, so setting an artificially low threshold could be problematic for purposes of addressing grid reliability.

bins.name: Joint Load and Generation Study
bins.desc: IECA fully supports simultaneous interconnection studies covering both a Data Center and its associated generation resource as offsetting loads, viewing this as the core mechanism for expedited approvals and efficient grid buildout.
quotes: We submit that a collaborative approach between States and the Commission can produce a process that can simultaneously study both a Data Center and a contractually-associated electric generation resource by joining both (i) the Federal approval of interconnection of the generation resource (through the Commission-regulated LGIP and LGIA process) and (ii) the State approval (through a State-regulated utility) of the interconnection of a new large load.

We fully support the proposal in the ANOPR of simultaneously studying the impacts on the network of one or more large loads (i.e., Data Centers) and one or more associated electric generating facilities as offsetting loads and resources and therefore worthy of expedited consideration and approval as a reasonable approach to addressing the interconnection of Data Centers that co-locate with existing or new electric generation facilities.

bins.name: Grid Backstop Rights for Hybrids
bins.desc: IECA supports system protection requirements but insists that hybrid interconnection rules must preserve the Data Center's ability to draw from the grid when its co-located generator goes offline, to avoid forcing Data Centers into fully islanded micro-grid operation.
quotes: we support requirements to install system protection facilities to prevent unauthorized injections or withdrawals, but we also recognize and support the ability of Data Centers relying on the grid as a backstop source of electric energy when a co-located generator experiences a full or partial outage.

bins.name: Outage Contingency and Grid Protection
bins.desc: IECA supports system protection facilities and highlights open questions about what happens to grid reliability during full or partial outages of either the Data Center or generator. It defers to NERC on bulk electric system reliability standards and threshold sizing.
quotes: some provision should be made for what will happen when the resource (i.e., generator) or the load (i.e., the Data Center) experiences a full or partial outage, whether scheduled or unscheduled.

we support requirements to install system protection facilities to prevent unauthorized injections or withdrawals, but we also recognize and support the ability of Data Centers relying on the grid as a backstop source of electric energy when a co-located generator experiences a full or partial outage.

Reliability and resource adequacy issues are affected much more by loads of 100 MW or larger, so setting an artificially low threshold could be problematic for purposes of addressing grid reliability.

bins.name: Expedited Interconnection for Speed to Market
bins.desc: IECA frames the entire rulemaking around speed to market and keeping Data Center investment onshore, endorsing expedited review for curtailable and dispatchable facilities and arguing that expedited access should not be restricted by size.
quotes: we believe the public interest is best served by creating a regulatory framework in which large loads, focusing primarily on large Data Centers, can be developed expeditiously in the U.S. to ensure that: (A) Data Center development does not move offshore to other countries with a more Data Center-friendly regulatory environment; and (B) the U.S. continues to be a leader in the global race to both develop and implement AI and other advanced computing capabilities.

for purposes of expediting interconnection to the transmissions system (i.e., enhancing "speed to market"), we submit that expedited review and approval should be available to all loads (i.e., all Data Centers), not just large Data Centers in excess of any particular size.

bins.name: Applicant-Pays Network Upgrades
bins.desc: IECA supports the applicant-pays principle, requiring Data Centers and associated generators to bear 100% of network upgrade costs, with a crediting mechanism mirroring existing generator interconnection rules.
quotes: we support requiring load and hybrid facilities, including any associated electric generating facilities not sharing an interconnection point with its associated Data Center, to bear all the costs of network upgrades required for their interconnection(s) and we support offsetting such costs through a crediting mechanism as is applicable under the Commissions interconnection rules applicable to generators.

bins.name: Joint Network Impact Study
bins.desc: IECA backs simultaneous network impact studies for co-located load and generation to reduce upgrade costs and enable expedited approval.
quotes: We fully support the proposal in the ANOPR of simultaneously studying the impacts on the network of one or more large loads (i.e., Data Centers) and one or more associated electric generating facilities as offsetting loads and resources and therefore worthy of expedited consideration and approval as a reasonable approach to addressing the interconnection of Data Centers that co-locate with existing or new electric generation facilities.

bins.name: Cost Responsibility for Load Interconnection
bins.desc: IECA endorses full cost responsibility for interconnecting parties, consistent with the generator cost allocation model, with grid benefits from new generation recognized as offsets.
quotes: we support requiring load and hybrid facilities, including any associated electric generating facilities not sharing an interconnection point with its associated Data Center, to bear all the costs of network upgrades required for their interconnection(s) and we support offsetting such costs through a crediting mechanism as is applicable under the Commissions interconnection rules applicable to generators.

bins.name: Co-location Study and Incentives
bins.desc: IECA supports co-location of Data Centers with generation as the preferred pathway, favoring joint federal-state study processes modeled on the DCC BYOG Pathway for regions experiencing delays.
quotes: We submit that a collaborative approach between States and the Commission can produce a process that can simultaneously study both a Data Center and a contractually-associated electric generation resource by joining both (i) the Federal approval of interconnection of the generation resource (through the Commission-regulated LGIP and LGIA process) and (ii) the State approval (through a State-regulated utility) of the interconnection of a new large load.

We fully support the proposal in the ANOPR of simultaneously studying the impacts on the network of one or more large loads (i.e., Data Centers) and one or more associated electric generating facilities as offsetting loads and resources and therefore worthy of expedited consideration and approval as a reasonable approach to addressing the interconnection of Data Centers that co-locate with existing or new electric generation facilities.

bins.name: Controllable Dispatch Policy Guidelines
bins.desc: IECA supports developing Commission policy guidelines for controllable dispatch applied to Data Centers and co-located generators, enabling real-time coordination with grid operators during outage events.
quotes: We support the use of "Controllable Dispatch" and other technological means of managing outages or derates of either a Data Center or its associated generation facility. Commission consideration in this or another proceeding of policy guidelines for applications of "Controllable Dispatch" to Data Centers and co-located generation facilities could be beneficial.

bins.name: PJM Interconnection Delays
bins.desc: IECA identifies PJM as the primary region experiencing significant large load interconnection delays, citing the DCC BYOG Pathway as an example of a targeted cross-jurisdictional remedy being developed there.
quotes: In the grid administered by the PJM Interconnection LLC ("PJM"), for example, interconnections of large loads are experiencing significant delays.

bins.name: U.S. AI Leadership Rationale
bins.desc: IECA frames expedited interconnection as essential to preventing Data Center development from moving offshore and maintaining U.S. leadership in AI and advanced computing, consistent with the Trump administration AI Action Plan.
quotes: we believe the public interest is best served by creating a regulatory framework in which large loads, focusing primarily on large Data Centers, can be developed expeditiously in the U.S. to ensure that: (A) Data Center development does not move offshore to other countries with a more Data Center-friendly regulatory environment; and (B) the U.S. continues to be a leader in the global race to both develop and implement AI and other advanced computing capabilities.

bins.name: Collaborative Federal-State Approach
bins.desc: IECA urges all parties to stop fighting over jurisdictional boundaries and instead work together on cross-jurisdictional study processes, citing the DCC BYOG Pathway in PJM as a model.
quotes: Rather than spending valuable time fighting between the Commission and State regulators over jurisdictional boundaries, we urge all sides to work together to recognize and remedy those circumstances in which the process of interconnecting large loads to the interstate transmission system under State laws is taking an inordinate amount of time and seek ways to expedite the process for interconnecting large loads to the grid.

We submit that a collaborative approach between States and the Commission can produce a process that can simultaneously study both a Data Center and a contractually-associated electric generation resource by joining both (i) the Federal approval of interconnection of the generation resource (through the Commission-regulated LGIP and LGIA process) and (ii) the State approval (through a State-regulated utility) of the interconnection of a new large load.

bins.name: No Mandatory One-Size-Fits-All Rule
bins.desc: IECA opposes imposing a mandatory federal interconnection process nationwide, arguing the Southeast and parts of the West are succeeding without delays and that a blanket rule would produce unintended consequences and slow successful markets.
quotes: we also urge the Commission to recognize regional differences and not impose a new mandatory interconnection process on those regions experiencing rapid load growth from large loads, which are not experiencing interconnection challenges or delays.

Conversely, we also recognize that in the Southeast and some areas in the West a significant amount of Data Center load growth is occurring without the same large load interconnection delays facing other areas. In those States, there is no compelling reason to alter the already successful large load interconnection processes for Data Centers in those States.

we suggest that any load interconnection program that the Commission may introduce should not be mandatory across the entirety of the U.S., i.e., a one-size-fits-all approach is not the best way forward.

bins.name: Co-location Outage Rules Unresolved
bins.desc: IECA raises a series of open questions about what rules should govern grid impacts when either a co-located generator or a Data Center experiences a scheduled or unscheduled outage, calling for these to be addressed in the rulemaking.
quotes: some provision should be made for what will happen when the resource (i.e., generator) or the load (i.e., the Data Center) experiences a full or partial outage, whether scheduled or unscheduled.

bins.name: Controllable Dispatch as Grid Tool
bins.desc: IECA supports policies enabling controllable dispatch of Data Center loads to manage transient load swings and outage events, viewing it as a shift away from 100% redundancy toward smarter grid integration.
quotes: We support the use of "Controllable Dispatch" and other technological means of managing outages or derates of either a Data Center or its associated generation facility. Commission consideration in this or another proceeding of policy guidelines for applications of "Controllable Dispatch" to Data Centers and co-located generation facilities could be beneficial.

bins.name: Targeted Relief for Delayed Regions
bins.desc: IECA supports targeted intervention in regions like PJM where delays are documented, while opposing mandatory reform in regions that are functioning well.
quotes: In the grid administered by the PJM Interconnection LLC ("PJM"), for example, interconnections of large loads are experiencing significant delays.
